title: Tour of the Italian Military Cemetery of Honour in Frankfurt

textContents.body:
Welcome to our tour to the Ita­lian Military Ceme­tery of Honour in Frankfurt.

You are now entering West­hau­sen Ceme­tery. West­hau­sen is a district of Frankfurt am Main that is unk­nown even to many of the city’s citizens. It sprang up between 1929 and 1931 as a con­struc­tion pro­ject in the late phase of so cal­led “New Frankfurt”, a pro­ject aimed at pro­viding func­tio­nal, affor­dable hou­sing. The ceme­tery was an even later deve­lopment: It was not until June 1950 that the first burials took place here. It also hou­ses the Ita­lian War Ceme­tery, which we are now going to explore together.

title: Tour del cimitero militare d'onore di Francoforte
subtitle: Rundgang auf Italienisch

textContents.body:
Il cimitero di West­hau­sen

Buongiorno e benve­n­uti in visita al cimitero milit­are ita­liano d’onore di Francoforte.

Ci troviamo all’ingresso del cimitero di West­hau­sen. West­hau­sen è un quar­tiere di Francoforte che anche per gli stessi abitanti della città è poco cono­sciuto. Il quar­tiere, costruito tra il 1929 e il 1931, fu parte del pro­gramma di espan­sione urba­nis­tica chiamato “Nuova Francoforte”, pro­getto che pun­tava a realiz­zare su larga scala alloggi econo­mici, moderni e funzio­nali. Il cimitero è più recente. È ent­rato in funzione solo a par­tire dal giu­gno 1950. Una parte ospita anche il cimitero milit­are ita­liano d’onore che è l’oggetto della nos­tra visita.

title: Route of remembrance: Frankfurt Messe and the NS
subtitle: A city tour about the November pogroms of 1938
description: A project by Messe Frankfurt and students of the Frankfurt University of Applied Science

textContents.body: The Festhalle in Frankfurt is a cen­tral event loca­tion stee­ped in over one hund­red years of his­tory. Offi­ci­ally opened in 1909, it has played host to countless cultu­ral, sporting and political events. During the November pogroms of 1938, the Festhalle served as a collec­tion point for Jewish men before they were sent to con­cen­t­ra­tion camps. The Festhalle suffered severe damage during the Second World War but was rebuilt afterwards. Today, it is a venue for inter­na­tio­nal con­certs, trade fairs and sporting events. The Festhalle repres­ents cultu­ral diver­sity, his­to­rical reflec­tion and pro­gress in the city of Frankfurt.

We, four students from the Frankfurt Uni­ver­sity of App­lied Sci­ences, have deve­l­o­ped a spe­cial city tour together with Messe Frankfurt. It starts at the Festhalle Frankfurt and leads to the for­mer main syn­agogue near the Kon­stablerwa­che.

Our aim is to make the indi­vidual fates of those per­secu­ted during the November pogroms of 1938 visi­ble and to cont­ribute to clarifying and com­ing to terms with this period.


Visi­tors can use the naviga­tion sys­tem or the map to explore the sta­ti­ons inde­pendently and expe­ri­ence his­tory at first hand. It is also possi­ble to pause the tour at will and conti­nue at the same place at ano­ther time. Of course, indi­vidual rou­tes can also be travel­led by public transport and sta­ti­ons can be skip­ped or visited exclusively digi­tally.

Transla­ted with DeepL.com (free ver­sion)

title: Frankfurt and National Socialism
subtitle: Overview tour from the Historical Museum Frankfurt to Börneplatz
description: Overview tour from the Historical Museum to Börneplatz

textContents.body:
Welcome to the “Frankfurt and Natio­nal Socialism” tour

The tour lasts about 60 min­u­tes. It will take you through the old town to Bör­ne­platz Square, stopping at eight different sites. On the way, you will learn about how the people of Frankfurt adop­ted the Natio­nal Socialist worldview and what con­sequen­ces that had for those who were excluded from it. You will also gain insights into the role played by the city gover­nment under Natio­nal Socialism and get to know the per­spec­tives of victims of per­secu­tion and of people who resis­ted the regime. Ano­ther topic will be the his­tory of the post-war efforts to address the Nazi injus­ti­ces and the ques­tion of how to come to terms with them today.

The His­to­rical Museum also cri­tically exami­nes its own his­tory as an insti­tu­tion that pro­fited from the per­secu­tion of Jews under Natio­nal Socialism. In those years it enri­ched its collec­tion with obje­cts previously in Frankfurt’s syn­agogues and the Museum of Jewish Antiquities. The obje­cts were acquired for the collec­tion under the pre­text of “rescuing them from destruc­tion”.

In many muse­ums, a “proven­ance rese­arch” department has been establis­hed to inves­tigate and recon­struct the his­tory of the obje­cts to determine to whe­ther or not they were acquired by unlawful means. As far back as the late 1940s, the His­to­rical Museum tur­ned over most of the pro­perty from the so-cal­led “rescue ope­ra­tion” to the Jewish Cultu­ral Recon­struc­tion orga­niza­t­ion—but not all of it. When the museum embarked on its sys­tema­tic pro­ven­ance rese­arch efforts in 2010, more stolen obje­cts tur­ned up. These obje­cts were given to the Jewish Museum and the Jewish com­mu­nity in 2018.

title: Not without us about us!
subtitle: Remembering Nazi-Euthanasia at the Main Cemetery Burial Site
description: Remembering Nazi Euthanasia at the Main Cemetery Burial Site

steps.title: Introduction: Main entrance of Main Cemetery

textContents.body:
This is the Main Ceme­tery of Frankfurt.
We are at the main entrance to the ceme­tery.
At this ceme­tery there is a memorial.
The memorial is for remembering victims of Natio­nal Socialism.
It remembers espe­cially people with disa­bilities.
We will show you the way to the memorial.
There you will rece­ive further informa­tion from us.

Before we go to the memorial, we want to explain what Nazi ‘eutha­na­sia’ murders were.
The Nazis kil­led a lot of people.
The Nazis also kil­led people with disa­bilities.
The Nazis also kil­led people with men­tal ill­nes­ses.

The murder of these people has a difficult name.
Today we call it Nazi ‘eutha­na­sia’ cri­mes.
Ever­yone should know:
They were murders.

metadata.author: Selbstvertreterrat Lebenshilfe Frankfurt am Main e. V.

metadata.title: Einleitung Portal Hauptfriedhof
metadata.author: Selbstvertreterrat Lebenshilfe Frankfurt am Main e.V.

title: Spurensuche mit dem Stift
subtitle: Ein Zeichen-Rundgang mit Künstlerin Katharina Müller
description: Ein Zeichen-Rundgang mit Künstlerin Katharina Müller

textContents.body:
Herzlich Will­kommen zur zeich­neri­schen Spuren­su­che.
Mach dich mit mir auf den Weg zu zwei Orten des Erinnerns. Ich möchte diese gemein­sam mit dir auf eine neue Art ­wahr­neh­men, durch das Zeich­nen.
Pack Stifte und Papier ein, dann kann es losge­hen.

Wow, ganz schön laut hier. Ich stehe an der Ampel an der Schil­ler­schule, die Straßen­bahn bimmelt, Autos hupen, eine Kranken­wa­gensirene rauscht vor­bei…

Auf der ande­ren Straßen­seite an der Kreuzung Hans-Thoma-Straße Ecke Gar­ten­straße befindet sich der noch rela­tiv neue, 2017 ein­geweihte „Platz der ver­ge­s­se­nen Kinder“.

Zwi­schen zwei Bäumen steht die Bron­ze­skulp­tur von Filippa Petter­son „der Drei­del“. Er erinnert an ein jüdi­sches Kinder­heim und an die Kinder, die wäh­rend des Natio­nal­so­zialismus hier leben muss­ten. Und auch an die Men­schen, die sich um sie gekümmert haben. Das und alle Informa­tio­nen erfahre ich über eine Info­tafel. Noch wei­tere Hin­ter­gründe kön­nen dort über QR-Codes abge­ru­fen wer­den.

title: Was hat der Nationalsozialismus mit mir zu tun?
subtitle: Ein Rundgang für Jugendliche und Familien
description: Ein Rundgang für Jugendliche und Familien

textContents.body:
Wie konnte es zum Natio­nal­so­zialismus und den Ver­bre­chen kommen, die wäh­rend dieser Zeit began­gen wur­den? Diese Frage beschäf­tigt heute viele Men­schen. Dazu gehören zum Bei­spiel Geschichtsfor­scher*innen, aber auch Men­schen, die sich generell für Geschichte inter­es­sie­ren. Viel­leicht, weil in ihrer Fami­lie Men­schen waren, die in der Zeit gelebt haben oder sogar ver­folgt wur­den. Besonders viele Men­schen beschäf­tigen sich mit der NS-Zeit, weil sie nicht möch­ten, dass sol­che Ver­bre­chen wieder gesche­hen oder weil sie Unge­rech­tigkeit nicht hin­neh­men möch­ten und gegen Benach­tei­ligung kämp­fen. Daher finden viele Frankfur­ter*innen es auch sehr wich­tig, sich mit der Geschichte der eige­nen Stadt aus­ein­anderzu­set­zen.

Das wol­len wir gemein­sam tun: Wir möch­ten jetzt gerne an drei Orten in der Frankfur­ter Alt­stadt einen Über­blick geben, was der Natio­nal­so­zialismus für Frankfurt bedeu­tet hat.

Such dir gerne einen Erwachse­nen, den du kennst und dem du ver­traust, dann könnt ihr euch gemein­sam auf Spuren­su­che bege­ben und euch aus­tauschen. Gerne kannst du noch Freund*innen und Mit­schüler*innen ein­laden, mit zu kommen.

title: Journey to the past: Jewish life in Westend 100 years ago
subtitle: A project of the Protestant Reformed Church

textContents.body: Welcome to our tour through a spe­cial period of Jewish life here in the Wes­tend. At various sta­ti­ons you will see what Jewish life used to be like here. Many things have been forgot­ten by many people.This includes the syn­agogue that once stood here at Unter­lindau 23. The con­fes­sio­nals of the Pro­tes­tant Reformed congrega­tion have spent a long time recon­structing the syn­agogue. We now want to start the tour with them.

title: “War Graves” – The Graves of Victims of War and Tyranny
subtitle: Tour on Frankfurt’s Main Cemetery

textContents.body:
“War Graves” on the Main Ceme­tery

Hallo – and a very warm welcome to our tour on Frankfurt’s Main Ceme­tery. With over 60,000 graves, this is the city’s lar­gest ceme­tery. Opened in 1828, it now covers 70 hecta­res – the equivalent of around 105 soc­cer pit­ches.

In the Main Ceme­tery’s eas­tern sec­tion, there are large burial grounds with grave markers in a unified design. Close to 6,700 people are buried here in the “War Graves” sec­tion. The dead are men, women, and child­ren from Germany and other coun­tries who lost their lives in the World Wars of the twenti­eth cen­tury or were victims of the reign of violence under the Nazi regime.

We – the Hesse State Asso­cia­tion in the German War Graves Commis­sion – have rese­ar­ched the his­tory of the graves in these sec­tions of the burial grounds. In this pro­cess, we took the fate of 32 indi­vidu­als buried here as exem­plary – and now on this tour we invite you to visit the graves of some of these dead and learn more about our rese­arch findings.

The tour takes approximately 90 min­u­tes in total. The direc­tions in the app are designed to help you move inde­pendently from one stop to the next. You can inter­rupt your tour at any time and conti­nue later at the same or a different audio point.

Hier findest du  Erklärun­gen für:

- wie funk­tio­niert der Spaziergang?
- wie kann ich den Spaziergang anhören?

Anhören und Anhal­ten

Kli­cke auf die­ses Symbol, um dir den Spaziergang anzuhören oder ihn anzuhal­ten.

Zeitleiste

Hier siehst du, wie lange gespro­chen wird.

Vor und Zurück

Mit die­sen Symbolen kannst du zum nächs­ten Ort wechseln.
Du kannst auch einen Ort zurück gehen.

Mein Stand­ort

Kli­cke auf die­ses Symbol, um dei­nen eige­nen Stand­ort auf der Karte zu sehen. 

Dafür muss das GPS auf deinem Gerät an sein.

title: DP-Camp Zeilsheim
subtitle: The Displaced Persons Camp for Holocaust Survivors
description: A Tour of Frankfurt’s Zeilsheim District

textContents.body:
Welcome!

Hello and welcome to the digi­tal guided tour on the his­tory of the Zeils­heim DP Camp. The tour begins at the Saalbau Stadt­halle Zeils­heim on the cor­ner of Bech­ten­wald­strasse and Hahnberg­weg.

title: Dancing out of the line
subtitle: A tour around the Ring of Statues
description: Beauty ideals in the Nazi era and today

textContents.body:
Welcome to the tour “Dancing Out of line” at the sculp­ture group Ring of Sta­tues in Rothschild Park.

This inter­ac­tive audio walk invi­tes you to discover tra­ces of the Nazi era in public spaces and cri­tically examine how ideals of beauty, images of power, and social norms conti­nue to have an impact today.

The focus is on the group of figu­res “Ring of Sta­tues” by sculp­tor Georg Kolbe, which was created during the Natio­nal Socialist era. Based on these sculp­tu­res, the tour opens up new per­spec­tives on his­tory, body images, and the culture of remem­brance. Through sound, move­ment, and reflec­tive ques­ti­ons, issues of beauty, iden­tity, and responsi­bility come to life and can be expe­ri­en­ced per­so­nally.

The star­ting point for this walk is the neo-Got­hic tower in Rothschild Park. Stand in front of the tower so that the high-rise building with the red UBS logo is visi­ble behind it.

Put on your headpho­nes, choose someone to count down from three so that you can all click on to the next sta­tion at the same time – and off you go: Three, two, one... go!

title: Forced Labor in Gallus and the Katzbach Concentration Camp
subtitle: A tour of Frankfurt's Gallus district
description: A tour of Frankfurt's Gallus district

textContents.body:
Hello a warm welcome to our audio tour on forced labor and the Katzbach sub­camp, a con­cen­t­ra­tion camp in Frankfurt’s Gallus district.

On this tour, you’ll hear about Nazi cri­mes that were not com­mitted in secret, but amidst the city’s daily life. These cri­mes included the forced labor of civi­lians and pri­so­ners-of-war in this district’s factories and compa­nies, the expro­pria­tion of Jewish busi­nes­ses, as well as the con­cen­t­ra­tion camp set up in 1944 here in the Adler­werke – the Adler Works – a large armaments pro­du­cer.

The nine stops on this tour take you from the Gallus­warte tower to the Adler­werke His­to­rical Site, a commemo­ra­tive and educa­tio­nal cen­ter. If you want to find out more about the topics on the tour and you’re here during the cen­ter’s opening hours, it’s well worth a visit after our tour.

___

The Gallus district lies to the west of Frankfurt’s inner city. It deve­l­o­ped in the late 19th cen­tury as the result of two major con­struc­tion pro­je­cts – Frankfurt Main Sta­tion to the south and the freight sta­tion to the nor­theast. When factories were built on the farm­land between these two sta­ti­ons, a new well-con­nected urban district was created – an indus­trial and workers’ quar­ter.

With its shops, a medi­cal practice and a pharmacy, the square around the Gallus­warte tower became the district’s econo­mic cen­ter. Hou­sing esta­tes, such as the Hel­ler­hof­sied­lung, were con­structed further to the west.

Since the district became renow­ned for its politically active workforce, it was also dub­bed the “red Gallus.” Many here were orga­nized in uni­ons and life was arranged with the help of labor move­ment insti­tu­ti­ons, from child­care to ven­ues for sports and leisure activities for young people, and a workers’ choir.

In 1932, alt­hough over half the Gallus residents voted for the Social Democ­rats and left-wing par­ties, there was also strong sup­port for the Nazi Party. This polarized district became the site of street fights. From 1933, when the Nazi Party came to power, workers attempt­ing to resist were per­secu­ted, arrested and inter­ned.

The Gallus city­scape was domi­nated by large factories. These included slipper manu­factu­rer J. & C.A. Schnei­der, nick­named “Schlappeschnei­der,” a major spon­sor of the Ein­tracht Frankfurt soc­cer club. Other large compa­nies here were Alfred Teves, a bra­king sys­tem manu­factu­rer, and Max Braun, known today for razors and watch design.

Many busi­nes­ses in Gallus swit­ched to pro­du­cing armaments for the Nazi regime’s war of conquest; for instance, a radio manu­factu­rer began pro­du­cing radio equipment for the military. The war ensured these compa­nies had well-fil­led order books. And some­thing else changed as well: Across the ent­ire city, camps were set up for forced labo­rers from the occu­p­ied territories.

One of the lar­gest compa­nies in Gallus was the Adler­werke, an engi­nee­ring works pro­du­cing machi­nes and vehi­cles. In the Second World War, it too pro­du­ced armaments. The Adler­werke used different types of forced labor. It not only used forced labo­rers for years from the occu­p­ied territories in Europe, but also had a con­cen­t­ra­tion camp set up on the works site in sum­mer 1944 – the Katzbach sub­camp, part of the Natz­weiler-Strut­hof con­cen­t­ra­tion camp complex.

Our tour focu­ses spe­cifically on the Adler­werke engi­nee­ring works. The first stop takes us to the for­mer factory buildings.

images.originalName: Station 1_Gallus Topografische Karte 1904_Hessisches Institut für Landesgeschichte(HIL).png

metadata.description: Historical topographical map of Gallus from 1904: The Gallus district is formed in the shape of a triangle between the tracks of the main passenger station (right) and those of the main freight station (top), which no longer exists today. Hessisches Institut für Landesgeschichte.
metadata.author: Königl. Preuss. Landesaufnahme
metadata.copyright: Georeferenzierung und Bereitstellung: Hessisches Institut für Landesgeschichte (HIL)

metadata.description: The consumer cooperative at Lahnstraße 5 was a place where workers in the Gallus district could organize themselves: members paid less for individual purchases, which helped to reduce costs. Institut für Stadtgeschichte, S7A 1998-12156.
metadata.author: unbekannt

textContents.body:
Herzlich Will­kommen zur Tour „Frankfurt und der Natio­nal­so­zialismus“

Der Rundgang wird ca. 60 Min­u­ten dau­ern. Sie kön­nen sich von Ort zu Ort navigie­ren. An jeder Sta­tion finden Sie außer­dem Ansich­ten in der Bildergale­rie. Der Rundgang führt Sie durch die Alt­stadt bis zum Bör­ne­platz, zu acht ver­schiede­nen Orten, an denen Sie erfah­ren, wie die Frankfur­ter*innen das natio­nal­so­zialis­ti­sche Welt­bild annah­men und wel­che Folgen das für diejenigen hatte, die nicht in das NS-Welt­bild pass­ten.

Sie wer­den über die Rolle der Stadt­ver­wal­tung im Natio­nal­so­zialismus informiert und ler­nen auch die Per­spek­tiven von Ver­folg­ten und Wider­ständigen ken­nen. Ein wei­te­res Thema wird die Geschichte der Auf­arbei­tung von NS-Unrecht sein und die Frage, wie wir uns heute damit aus­ein­ander­set­zen kön­nen.

Auch das His­tori­sche Museum Frankfurt befasst sich kri­tisch mit der eige­nen Geschichte, denn es hat auch wäh­rend des Natio­nal­so­zialismus von der Ver­folgung von Jüdin­nen und Juden pro­fi­tiert. Es hat seine Samm­lung um Stü­cke berei­chert, die sich zuvor in Frankfur­ter Syn­agogen oder dem Museum für Jüdi­sche Alter­tü­mer befanden.
Die Objekte wur­den unter dem Vor­wand der „Ret­tung vor Zer­störung“ in die Muse­ums­samm­lung auf­genommen.

Heute forscht in vie­len Museen die „Pro­ve­ni­en­zfor­schung“ dazu, die Geschichte der Samm­lungs­objekte zu erkunden und zu ermit­teln, ob sich unrechtmäßig erworbene Gegen­stände darin befinden. Das His­tori­sche Museum hat die meis­ten Objekte aus der räuberi­schen „Ret­tungs­ak­tion“ bereits Ende der 1940er Jahre an die Orga­ni­sa­tion „Jewish Cultu­ral Recon­struc­tion“ resti­tu­iert - aber nicht voll­ständig. Denn als das Museum 2010 mit der sys­tema­ti­schen Pro­ve­ni­en­zfor­schung begann, tauchte wei­te­res Raubgut auf, das 2018 an das Jüdi­sche Museum und die Jüdi­sche Gemeinde über­ge­ben wurde.

Welcome to a tour of Gries­heim’s his­tory in the Natio­nal Socialist period.

Until 1928, Gries­heim was an inde­pendent rural muni­ci­pa­lity with a popula­tion of approximately 12,500. It was not incor­po­r­ated—that is, it did not become a district of Frankfurt—­until five years before the Nazi acces­sion to power. Our tour takes us through the his­to­rical town cen­ter, today cal­led Old Gries­heim. In the period we are revi­sit­ing, there were numerous shops, restau­rants, small busi­nes­ses, and a vibrant network of various clubs and asso­cia­ti­ons here. Accor­dingly, nearly ever­yone knew ever­yone else.

From 1933 onward, the per­secu­tion of those who stood in the way of Natio­nal Socialism took place in plain sight here. Com­mu­nists, Social Democ­rats, and labor union members were arrested. The long­standing head of the Gries­heim library was dismissed because the Nazis considered him politically unre­liable. A number of Gries­heim’s school teachers suffered the same fate. Jews were under pressure to give up their shops and homes. Jehovah’s Wit­nes­ses and homo­sexu­als in the com­mu­nity were put in pri­sons and con­cen­t­ra­tion camps.

After World War II began, Gries­heim’s men were incre­asin­gly drafted for military service. At the same time, ever more forced labo­rers from various coun­tries arrived in the town. Many were given lodgings in private homes and worked in family-ope­rated busi­nes­ses such as bake­ries and gar­de­ning shops. Thousands of others were com­pel­led to reside in fenced-in barrack camps, vacant indus­trial buildings, and shut­tered restau­rants. Forced labor—and the suffering asso­ciated with it—were present ever­ywhere in Gries­heim.

metadata.description: Marienanlage Park with the mayor's house, which later became the “Brown House of Griesheim”
metadata.author: Verlag Georg Emrich, Griesheim
